questions.stem: Which type of Multiple Sclerosis is characterized by a steady worsening of symptoms from onset, without distinct relapses or remissions?
questions.rationale: Primary progressive MS (PPMS) is characterized by a steady worsening of neurological function from onset, without distinct relapses or remissions. This accounts for approximately 10-15% of MS cases.

questions.stem: A patient presents with a first clinical episode suggestive of MS. MRI shows disseminated lesions in time and space. What is this most likely to be diagnosed as?
questions.rationale: Clinically Isolated Syndrome (CIS) refers to a first episode of neurologic symptoms lasting at least 24 hours, caused by inflammation or demyelination. If MRI shows dissemination in space and time, the risk of developing MS is very high.

questions.stem: Which phase of MS is characterized by a transition from relapsing-remitting disease to steady progression with or without occasional minor remissions?
questions.rationale: Secondary Progressive MS (SPMS) typically follows an initial relapsing-remitting course. Over time, the disease begins to progress more steadily, with or without occasional relapses or minor remissions.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old female patient with RRMS for 8 years presents with progressive worsening of ambulation and cognitive function over the past 2 years, with no clear relapses. MRI shows new T2 lesions but no gadolinium enhancement. What is the most appropriate diagnosis?
questions.rationale: This patient transitioned from RRMS to SPMS after 8 years and now shows progressive disability without clinical relapses. The absence of gadolinium enhancement indicates inactive disease, making this Inactive SPMS (progressive without relapses).

questions.stem: Which environmental factor is most strongly associated with increased MS risk?
questions.rationale: Vitamin D deficiency is the most strongly established environmental risk factor for MS. Prevalence correlates with geographic latitude (less sun exposure), and low vitamin D levels are associated with higher MS risk.

questions.stem: Which gene is most strongly associated with MS susceptibility?
questions.rationale: The HLA-DRB1*15:01 allele is the strongest genetic risk factor for MS, located in the MHC class II region on chromosome 6. Carriers have 3x increased MS risk.
